perturbation.original_text: 1.1 The following words shall have the following meanings throughout this Agreement: a) "Agreement" means this Agreement and all Schedules annexed hereto, as amended from time to time by the Parties; b) "Freight" means any goods directed to one Party by the other for pick up and/or delivery; c) "Force Majeure" means i) An Act of God; ii) A strike, lock out or other labour disturbance; 2 - 2 - iii) A war, revolution, insurrection, riot, blockade or any other unlawful act against public order authority; iv) A storm, fire, flood, explosion, lightening or other hazardous weather condition; v) Any Ministry of Transportation road closure or other acts of government or transport authorities which are beyond the control of the Parties; vi) Any air traffic control delays, cancellations, reroutes or other acts of government, airport or aviation authorities, which are beyond the control of the Parties; vii) Any loss, hijacking, government seizure or diversion.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.1 The following words shall have the following meanings throughout this Agreement: a) "Agreement" means this Agreement and all Schedules annexed hereto, as amended from time to time by the Parties; b) "Freight" means any goods directed to one Party by the other for pick up and/or delivery; c) "Force Majeure" means events that may delay the party to perform the respective duties. It includes: i) An Act of God; ii) A strike, lock out or other labour disturbance; 2 - 2 - iii) A war, revolution, insurrection, riot, blockade or any other unlawful act against public order authority; iv) A storm, fire, flood, explosion, lightening or other hazardous weather condition; v) Any Ministry of Transportation road closure or other acts of government or transport authorities which are beyond the control of the Parties; vi) Any air traffic control delays, cancellations, reroutes or other acts of government, airport or aviation authorities, which are beyond the control of the Parties; vii) Any loss, hijacking, government seizure or diversion.
perturbation.explanation: By altering the definition of 'Force Majeure' to 'means events that may delay the party to perform the respective duties', we introduce ambiguity. The original definition lists specific events. The new definition broadens the scope, potentially including events that cause mere delay but not necessarily preventing performance entirely. This contradiction arises specifically in Section 3.4, which states that each Party must provide services at an on-time performance level, 'excluding delays caused by the other Party or events of Force Majeure.' The original specific definition allows clear determination if the delay is caused by Force Majeure. The altered definition, however, creates uncertainty, as any event causing delay could now be argued as Force Majeure, thus weakening the on-time performance obligation. Therefore, there's an in-text contradiction between these 2 sections of the contract.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Each Party agrees to provide the services outlined above at an on time performance level of no less than ninety percent (90%) of the scheduled delivery time, excluding delays caused by the other Party or events of Force Majeure. Monthly, the performance level shall be measured as set out above. Failure to provide services as set out herein constitutes a Monthly Service Failure.
perturbation.justification: The changed text broadens the definition of 'Force Majeure' from a list of specific events to 'events that may delay the party to perform the respective duties.' This creates ambiguity and conflicts with the contradicted text, which requires a specific on-time performance level, excluding delays caused by Force Majeure. The new definition allows almost any delay to be classified as Force Majeure, potentially nullifying the performance obligation. Therefore, the change introduces a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 5.5 Every invoice shall be reviewed by the invoiced Party and subject to paragraphs 5.2, shall pay the invoice net fifteen (15) days from the date of invoicing. Invoices shall be delivered to the invoiced Party within three (3) days of the date of invoicing. 6 - 6 - 5.6 Interest, at the prime rate then charged to commercial customers by the Canadian Imperil Bank of Commerce (Toronto Main Branch), shall be payable on all amounts overdue for thirty (30) days or more.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.5 Every invoice shall be reviewed by the invoiced Party and subject to paragraphs 5.2, shall pay the invoice net thirty (30) days from the date of invoicing. Invoices shall be delivered to the invoiced Party within three (3) days of the date of invoicing. 6 - 6 - 5.6 Interest, at the prime rate then charged to commercial customers by the Canadian Imperil Bank of Commerce (Toronto Main Branch), shall be payable on all amounts overdue for fifteen (15) days or more.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the payment term in section 5.5 from 'net fifteen (15) days' to 'net thirty (30) days' while simultaneously changing the interest accrual in section 5.6 from 'thirty (30) days or more' to 'fifteen (15) days or more' creates a direct conflict. The contract now stipulates that invoices are due in 30 days, but interest applies after only 15 days, creating a discrepancy on when interest must be applied and when invoices should be paid. This causes an in-text contradiction between when penalties should be applied to the invoice due date, and when is the set payment period.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Interest, at the prime rate then charged to commercial customers by the Canadian Imperil Bank of Commerce (Toronto Main Branch), shall be payable on all amounts overdue for fifteen (15) days or more.
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces a direct contradiction. Section 5.5 now states invoices are due in 30 days ('net thirty (30) days'), while section 5.6 states interest accrues after only 15 days ('fifteen (15) days or more'). This creates a clear inconsistency regarding the payment terms and penalty application, making it uncertain when interest should be applied according to the agreement and thus fulfilling the definition of 'Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: 17.1 For the purposes of this Agreement, the following shall constitute events of default by a Party (hereinafter referred to as "Events of Default"): (a) if a petition is filed against it under any applicable bankruptcy legislation and is not withdrawn or dismissed within sixty (60) days thereafter; (b) if a resolution is passed by it respecting the sale of all or substantially all of its assets, or an order for the winding up of its business is made, or it otherwise agrees to make a bulk sale of it's assets; (c) if it ceases or threatens to cease to carry on its business;
perturbation.changed_text: 17.1 For the purposes of this Agreement, the following shall constitute events of default by a Party (hereinafter referred to as "Events of Default"): (a) if a petition is filed against it under any applicable bankruptcy legislation and is not withdrawn or dismissed within thirty (30) days thereafter; (b) if a resolution is passed by it respecting the sale of all or substantially all of its assets, or an order for the winding up of its business is made, or it otherwise agrees to make a bulk sale of it's assets; (c) if it ceases or threatens to cease to carry on its business;
perturbation.explanation: Changing the period in Section 17.1(a) from 'sixty (60) days' to 'thirty (30) days' creates a conflict with Section 17.2(a), which specifies a 'one hundred and twenty (120) days written notice' period to terminate the agreement upon an Event of Default but on cases except 17.1 (g) and (h). Since 17.1(a) is not 17.1 (g) nor 17.1 (h), this implies that while a petition not withdrawn within 30 days constitutes an event of default, the non-defaulting party must still provide 120 days written notice before termination. This contradicts the idea that a default should immediately allow termination, creating uncertainty on when the agreement can be effectively terminated after an event of default. Therefore, these creates an in-text contradiction in when termination can be made, after a Default happens.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Upon the occurrence of an Event of Default and in addition to any rights or remedies available to it under this Agreement or at law or in equity, the Party not in default may exercise any or all of the following remedies: (a) terminate this Agreement, upon giving one hundred and twenty (120) days written notice, otherwise upon written notice with respect to 17.1 (g) and (h); (b) recover from the defaulting Party any and all monies then due and to become due; and (c) take possession, immediately, without demand or notice, without any court order or other process of law, any and all of its property (including bags and containers) and Freight received by the defaulting Party under this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the grace period for bankruptcy filings from 60 to 30 days, triggering an event of default sooner. However, Section 17.2(a) still mandates a 120-day written notice for termination upon most Events of Default, including the bankruptcy scenario in 17.1(a). This creates a contradiction because while a default technically occurs after 30 days, the non-defaulting party is obligated to wait 120 days before terminating, rendering the immediate impact of the default uncertain and conflicting with the notion that a default should have immediate consequences, as noted in the provided 'Perturbation Explanation'. This aligns with the Ambiguities definition, creating difficulty in enforcing obligations.
